questions.scenario: You need to add a new compute node to the BCM cluster and assign it to the gpu-nodes category from the command line so the change can be scripted.
questions.explanation: cmsh is the scripting-friendly BCM shell — non-interactive flags and -c lets you add the node, assign the category, and commit in a single automated invocation.

whyNotOthers.reason: Base View is a click-through web UI, not scriptable for repeatable provisioning workflows
cmha manages head-node High Availability, not compute-node provisioning
cm-create-image builds the software image, not the node-to-category assignment

questions.scenario: An admin reports the BCM web dashboard is showing stale data after a recent change. They want to verify config sync between active and passive head nodes.
questions.explanation: cmha status surfaces head-node HA state, last sync time, and whether the standby is healthy — the right tool to validate active/passive coherence.

whyNotOthers.reason: cmsh can show config but does not directly expose HA sync state and replication lag
Base View shows the dashboard — but if it's stale, you need the HA tool to diagnose why
Image creation is unrelated to head-node HA state

questions.scenario: After applying a new NVIDIA driver, you want every compute node to receive it on next reboot via BCM provisioning.
questions.explanation: cm-create-image rebuilds (or updates) the software image used to provision nodes. After the image is updated, compute nodes pick up the new driver at the next provisioning boot.

whyNotOthers.reason: cmsh can point a node to a different image, but the image itself must be (re)built with cm-create-image first
Base View shows image assignments but does not build images
cmha is for HA control, not image content

questions.scenario: A new junior admin needs a visual cluster overview to see GPU utilization across nodes during onboarding.
questions.explanation: Base View is the browser-based BCM dashboard built for click-through cluster monitoring and is the ideal entry point for new admins to visualize the cluster.

whyNotOthers.reason: cmsh is a CLI — steeper learning curve and less visual
cmha is a narrow HA tool, not a general cluster overview
Image builder, no monitoring view

questions.scenario: A data scientist submitted a multi-node training job but it has been pending for 30 minutes. You need to see WHY it's waiting.
questions.explanation: squeue's REASON column (last column with %R) explains why a job is pending — Resources, Priority, ReqNodeNotAvail, QOSMaxGRESPerUser, etc. That is the diagnostic field.

whyNotOthers.reason: sbatch submits jobs; it does not show why an existing job is pending
sinfo shows node state — useful for ruling out drained nodes, but does not show job-specific reason
sacct shows accounting for past/finished jobs, not live queue reasons

questions.scenario: You need to drain node gpu-h100-04 for firmware maintenance without killing the running job on it.
questions.explanation: scontrol update NodeName=gpu-h100-04 State=DRAIN sets the node to DRAIN, which prevents new jobs from being scheduled there while allowing the current job to finish.

whyNotOthers.reason: sinfo is read-only — shows node state but cannot change it
sacctmgr manages accounting database (accounts, QoS, associations), not node state
squeue lists jobs in the queue, cannot change node state

questions.scenario: A new team needs to be onboarded with a 16-GPU quota and access to the ai partition.
questions.explanation: sacctmgr creates the account, associates users to it with GrpTRES limits (gpu=16), and grants partition access. It is the user/quota administration tool in Slurm.

whyNotOthers.reason: sbatch submits jobs; it does not configure quotas or accounts
scontrol manages live cluster objects (jobs, nodes, partitions), not the accounting/quota database
sacct queries past usage; it cannot create accounts or set quotas

questions.scenario: Last month's GPU usage report is due — broken down by account and including elapsed time, GPU TRES, and exit status.
questions.explanation: sacct reads the accounting database and supports --format=Account,Elapsed,AllocTRES,State to produce per-account GPU usage reports.

whyNotOthers.reason: sbatch is for submission, not reporting
squeue shows live jobs, not historical accounting
sinfo shows node/partition state, not user/account accounting

questions.scenario: You need to install the NVIDIA GPU Operator on a fresh Kubernetes cluster with default values.
questions.explanation: The GPU Operator is distributed as a Helm chart. helm install nvidia/gpu-operator is the recommended deployment path — kubectl alone cannot install the full chart with templated values.

whyNotOthers.reason: kubectl can apply rendered manifests but does not handle Helm chart templating, dependencies, or versioned releases
gpu-operator is what you are installing — it is not the installer
network-operator enables RDMA networking, not GPU support

questions.scenario: A pod requesting 1 GPU is stuck in Pending state — you suspect the device plugin isn't advertising GPU resources.
questions.explanation: kubectl describe node <name> shows the node's allocatable resources including nvidia.com/gpu. kubectl describe pod shows scheduling events. These are the first-line diagnostics.

whyNotOthers.reason: helm manages releases, not pod scheduling diagnostics
GPU Operator is the system being diagnosed — kubectl is the tool to inspect it
Network Operator is unrelated to GPU device plugin

questions.scenario: Inference pods need GPUDirect RDMA over InfiniBand for ultra-low-latency multi-pod communication.
questions.explanation: The NVIDIA Network Operator deploys MOFED, NV peer-memory, RDMA shared device plugin, and Multus — the stack required for GPUDirect RDMA from K8s pods.

whyNotOthers.reason: kubectl is the client CLI — the RDMA stack must be installed by the Network Operator first
helm installs the Network Operator chart, but the Network Operator is the component that provides the capability
GPU Operator handles compute (drivers, device plugin), not RDMA networking

questions.scenario: You want to validate that all nodes labeled for AI workloads have completed GPU Operator readiness checks.
questions.explanation: The GPU Operator stack includes the validation daemonset (nvidia-operator-validator) — its pods report success only when drivers, toolkit, device plugin, and CUDA validation all pass on a given node.

whyNotOthers.reason: kubectl queries the state but the validation logic comes from GPU Operator components
helm tracks release health, not per-node GPU readiness
Network Operator validates RDMA, not GPU compute readiness

questions.scenario: A data scientist wants to submit a single-GPU PyTorch training job to their team's Run:ai project from the command line.
questions.explanation: runai submit is the daily-driver Run:ai CLI command for training and interactive workloads — it handles auth, project context, image, and resource flags.

whyNotOthers.reason: Projects are configured by admins via the UI or CRDs — data scientists submit into them
Inference workloads use a different submit type — this is a training job
kubectl works but bypasses Run:ai's policy/quota enforcement — runai is the supported submission path

questions.scenario: Two teams need different GPU quotas (team-a: 32 GPUs, team-b: 16 GPUs) with team-a allowed to burst into team-b's idle GPUs.
questions.explanation: Run:ai Project CRDs hold quotas, over-quota policy, and department bindings — this is the resource the admin configures to express per-team GPU limits and burst behavior.

whyNotOthers.reason: The CLI submits workloads; quotas live in the Project resource
Inference resources do not define quotas — projects do
kubectl can edit the Project CRD, but the conceptual object is the Run:ai Project — the admin needs to know that abstraction

questions.scenario: An LLM inference endpoint should auto-scale from 0 to N replicas based on incoming request traffic and serve fractional GPUs.
questions.explanation: Run:ai inference workloads use scale-to-zero autoscaling (KServe/Knative under the hood) and support fractional GPU allocation — exactly the requirements stated.

whyNotOthers.reason: runai is the CLI umbrella — the specific submit type is the inference workload
Projects set policy; they do not define the workload type
kubectl can create the resource but the abstraction here is Run:ai's inference workload kind

questions.scenario: Run:ai workloads are pending, and you suspect the underlying Kubernetes scheduler is rejecting them — you need to inspect the raw pod events.
questions.explanation: When Run:ai workloads stall, dropping down to kubectl describe pod and kubectl get events surfaces the underlying K8s reason (Insufficient resources, Taint/Toleration mismatch, etc.) that Run:ai's UI may abbreviate.

whyNotOthers.reason: runai describe gives Run:ai-level state but not the full Kubernetes events
Project resource doesn't surface pod-level scheduler events
Inference resource doesn't expose raw scheduler events

questions.scenario: A developer wants to test an NGC PyTorch image locally on a single workstation before submitting to the cluster.
questions.explanation: Docker with the NVIDIA Container Toolkit is the standard single-node workflow for pulling and running NGC GPU containers interactively.

whyNotOthers.reason: Enroot is geared toward Slurm/HPC environments without root; overkill for single-workstation interactive testing
Pyxis is a Slurm plugin — it only runs inside a Slurm job context
ngc is the registry CLI for pulling/listing — actually running the container is docker's job

questions.scenario: A Slurm multi-node training job must run inside a container without requiring root on compute nodes.
questions.explanation: Pyxis is the Slurm SPANK plugin (backed by Enroot) that lets srun/sbatch launch containers with --container-image as the unprivileged user. It is the supported HPC pattern.

whyNotOthers.reason: Docker requires root or docker-group access and is not the HPC standard for multi-node containers under Slurm
Enroot is the runtime backend, but Pyxis is what integrates it with srun/sbatch — and the question asks for the Slurm-integrated answer
ngc is the registry CLI, not a runtime

questions.scenario: Authenticate to NGC and pull a private team-restricted NIM image into the local registry cache.
questions.explanation: The ngc CLI handles NGC authentication, org/team context, and listing/pulling private team images. After ngc login, docker pull also works, but ngc is the canonical registry tool.

whyNotOthers.reason: Docker pulls but does not handle NGC org/team auth or token rotation natively
Enroot imports images but does not provide NGC org/team auth flow
Pyxis is a Slurm runtime plugin — it does not authenticate to registries

questions.scenario: Convert a Docker image to a squashfs format suitable for unprivileged HPC container execution.
questions.explanation: enroot import converts a Docker/OCI image into an Enroot squashfs bundle that runs unprivileged — the standard HPC import step.

whyNotOthers.reason: Docker produces overlay-filesystem images, not Enroot squashfs bundles
Pyxis consumes Enroot bundles but does not produce them
ngc pulls but does not convert to Enroot format

questions.scenario: You need to partition an H100 GPU into 7 small MIG instances to serve many small inference replicas.
questions.explanation: nvidia-smi mig is the dedicated MIG sub-command for listing profiles, creating GPU Instances (GI) and Compute Instances (CI). It's the right tool for the partitioning step.

whyNotOthers.reason: nvidia-smi -mig 1 enables MIG mode, but partitioning is done through the mig sub-command
dcgmi monitors and diagnoses; it does not configure MIG partitions
dcgm-exporter publishes telemetry, not configuration

questions.scenario: Job start-up latency on a multi-tenant cluster is high because the GPU driver unloads between jobs.
questions.explanation: nvidia-smi -pm 1 enables persistence mode so the driver stays loaded even when no processes are active — eliminating the cold-start tax between jobs.

whyNotOthers.reason: MIG sub-command is for partitioning, not driver persistence
dcgmi can observe but does not set persistence mode
Exporter publishes metrics; cannot configure driver behavior

questions.scenario: You want a live 1-second-resolution dashboard of GPU utilization, memory, SM occupancy, and ECC errors during a training run.
questions.explanation: dcgmi dmon supports rich field IDs (utilization, memory, ECC, NVLink) at sub-second resolution — designed for live ops dashboards in the terminal.

whyNotOthers.reason: nvidia-smi dmon is similar but exposes fewer fields, especially fewer DCGM-specific metrics like ECC events
MIG sub-command is for partitioning, not monitoring
Exporter is for Prometheus scrape — not interactive terminal dashboards

questions.scenario: Prometheus needs to scrape GPU metrics from every node in the cluster for long-term dashboards.
questions.explanation: dcgm-exporter exposes DCGM metrics in Prometheus format — typically deployed as a daemonset via GPU Operator. It is the bridge from DCGM to your observability stack.

whyNotOthers.reason: Not a Prometheus exporter; would require a wrapper to parse output
MIG sub-command does not export metrics
dcgmi is interactive — not a /metrics endpoint

questions.scenario: Multi-GPU NCCL tests on a DGX H100 are getting only 1/8 the expected bandwidth — you suspect the NVSwitch fabric isn't fully initialized.
questions.explanation: On HGX/DGX systems, Fabric Manager must be running for NVSwitch to form the full NVLink mesh. systemctl status nvidia-fabricmanager is the first check when intra-node NCCL collapses to PCIe rates.

whyNotOthers.reason: ibstat checks InfiniBand ports between nodes — not NVLink within a node
Measures IB bandwidth between nodes, irrelevant for intra-node NVLink
Diagnoses InfiniBand subnet, not NVSwitch fabric

questions.scenario: A new compute node was added and you want to confirm both of its InfiniBand HCA ports are Active and at 400Gbps.
questions.explanation: ibstat lists each HCA port with State, Physical state, Rate, and LID — the canonical first command to verify IB port health and link rate.

whyNotOthers.reason: Fabric Manager is for NVSwitch (intra-node NVLink), not InfiniBand ports
Measures performance — but you first need to confirm the port is even Active
ibdiagnet works across the whole fabric; ibstat is the focused per-node check

questions.scenario: Acceptance testing requires you to validate the actual end-to-end RDMA write bandwidth between two new compute nodes.
questions.explanation: ib_write_bw is the canonical point-to-point RDMA write benchmark — run as a server on one node and client on the other to measure achieved bandwidth.

whyNotOthers.reason: Manages NVSwitch fabric, not IB benchmarks
ibstat shows port state but does not run traffic to measure throughput
ibdiagnet diagnoses fabric health but is not a bandwidth benchmark tool

questions.scenario: Post-installation, sporadic NCCL hangs are suspected to come from a flapping IB port somewhere in the fabric. You want a cluster-wide error report.
questions.explanation: ibdiagnet --pc scans the entire subnet, walks the routing tables, and reports port-counter errors across every switch and HCA — the right tool to find the rogue link.

whyNotOthers.reason: Out of scope — Fabric Manager is intra-node NVSwitch
ibstat is per-node; you would have to script it across the whole cluster — ibdiagnet does it natively
A bandwidth tester, not a fabric-wide diagnostic
